stories.headline: Astronomers Detected a Chirping Signal from an Exploding Star — and Have No Idea What Made It
stories.hook: A strange signal from a distant supernova is speeding up over time, like a chirp — and it doesn't match anything astronomers have seen before.
stories.body:
When astronomers pointed their instruments at a recently exploded star, they expected the familiar signatures of a supernova: a bright flash followed by a slow fade. Instead, they found something that had no name: a radio signal that accelerates in frequency over time, rising in pitch like a chirp. The pattern was immediately reminiscent of the gravitational waves detected when black holes collide, but this signal is electromagnetic — radio waves — and it appears to be coming from a completely different kind of event.

The discovery, made using radio telescope arrays across multiple continents, has stumped researchers. The leading hypotheses involve exotic compact objects — possibly two neutron stars spiraling toward each other inside the remnant of the explosion, or a rapidly spinning pulsar whose beam is being amplified by the surrounding supernova debris. The chirping pattern — a frequency that climbs steadily with time — is something no supernova remnant has produced in previous observations across the thousands of known examples in the galaxy.

The timing couldn't be more interesting. Astronomers are just beginning to map the diverse range of signals that exploding stars can produce, and many are now wondering whether chirping remnants are simply rare, or whether they've always existed and telescopes weren't sensitive enough to find them before. If the source turns out to be a neutron star merger happening inside a fresh supernova remnant, it would be an unprecedented event — a cosmic explosion inside a cosmic explosion, producing ripples of both light and gravity.

image.alt: A Hubble Space Telescope image showing a stellar merger model with pink and white regions against dark space
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Strange new signal from exploding star (space news)
Space.com: Latest supernova discoveries
NASA Astrophysics Division update, March 2026

stories.headline: Scientists Found Plant DNA That Has Survived Unchanged for 400 Million Years
stories.hook: Hidden inside modern plant genomes are ancient 'switches' that have been present since before the age of dinosaurs — and they still work.
stories.body:
Deep inside the genomes of modern plants, scientists have found what they're calling an enormous hidden archive: stretches of regulatory DNA that appear to have been conserved essentially unchanged for more than 400 million years. These aren't genes themselves — they don't code for proteins — but they act as molecular switches, turning other genes on and off at the right time and place during a plant's development. Their extraordinary age suggests they are essential to plant life in a way that evolution simply hasn't been able to improve upon.

The discovery emerged from comparing the genomes of hundreds of plant species across the evolutionary tree, from simple mosses and ferns to flowering plants. Researchers identified regions of DNA sequence that appear again and again across distantly related lineages — species that diverged hundreds of millions of years ago and have since evolved radically different forms, sizes, and adaptations. Normally, non-coding DNA accumulates random mutations quickly. The fact that these sequences have barely changed across such vast timescales means any mutations were eliminated by natural selection: changes were lethal, or at least strongly disadvantageous.

The implications are significant for our understanding of how complex plant development is orchestrated. These ancient switches appear to coordinate some of the most fundamental plant processes — cell division, tissue identity, response to light. They may also explain why attempts to engineer crop plants with improved traits sometimes have unexpected side effects: you're tugging on wiring that has been operating in essentially the same way for longer than animals have had backbones.

image.alt: A close-up photograph of a green leaf marked with brown spots, revealing intricate surface detail
image.credit: Photo by Wolfgang Hasselmann on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Biology — ancient plant DNA
Nature: Plant genomics research
Phys.org: Plant biology discoveries 2026

stories.headline: Scientists Finally Know What Caused Santorini's 28,000 Earthquakes
stories.hook: For weeks in early 2025, thousands of earthquakes shook Santorini daily — a new study has traced them to a sheet of magma as large as 200,000 Olympic swimming pools.
stories.body:
In late January 2025, the Greek island of Santorini and its neighboring islands began shaking. Not with the grinding slowness of tectonic movement, but in rapid, rhythmic bursts — sometimes dozens of earthquakes per hour. Over the following month, seismographs recorded more than 28,000 individual quakes, several exceeding magnitude 5.0. Residents evacuated, and geologists scrambled to understand what was happening beneath the seafloor.

Now, a new study published using data from an unprecedented combination of seafloor monitoring instruments, satellite radar measurements, GPS stations, and artificial intelligence analysis has pinpointed the cause. Approximately 300 million cubic meters of molten rock — enough to fill 200,000 Olympic swimming pools — rapidly rose from depth and stalled about four kilometers beneath the seafloor. As this vast sheet of magma, called a dike, pushed through the crust, it fractured surrounding rock and triggered the earthquake swarm. The AI-driven analysis allowed researchers to track the magma's path in near real-time, giving Greek authorities their first-ever granular picture of a volcanic crisis as it unfolded.

Perhaps the most surprising finding was a previously unknown connection between Santorini and Kolumbo, a submarine volcano located seven kilometers offshore. As magma moved, satellite data showed Santorini actually subsiding slightly — an effect explained by the hydraulic link between the two volcanic systems, which appear to share a deeper plumbing network. The crisis resolved without an eruption, but researchers say understanding how these twin systems communicate is now critical for assessing future risk at one of Europe's most visited destinations.

image.alt: A blue and purple spiral-shaped cosmic image suggesting swirling forces of energy
image.credit: Photo by Aron Visuals on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Massive magma surge sparked 28,000 Santorini earthquakes
National Geographic: Why Santorini kept shaking
AccuWeather: Hidden magma movements behind Santorini earthquake swarm

stories.headline: A CEO Asked ChatGPT How to Void a $250 Million Contract. The Court Was Not Impressed.
stories.hook: Rather than listen to his lawyers, the head of Korean gaming giant Krafton turned to an AI chatbot to help him duck a massive developer payout — and a Delaware court just made him pay for it.
stories.body:
When Krafton, the South Korean publisher behind the survival game PUBG, acquired Unknown Worlds Entertainment in 2021, the deal included a $250 million bonus contingent on strong sales of the studio's next game, Subnautica 2. As the game's release approached and internal projections suggested the payout was imminent, CEO Changhan Kim apparently decided he didn't want to honor the deal. Rather than rely on his legal team, Kim allegedly consulted ChatGPT to devise a strategy for avoiding the payout — using the AI to "contrive a corporate 'takeover' strategy" that would force out the studio's founder and nullify the bonus agreement.

A Delaware court saw through the scheme. In a ruling delivered this month, the judge not only rejected Krafton's arguments but ordered the reinstatement of the developer who had been terminated as part of the plan. The ruling highlighted how Kim had ignored formal legal counsel in favor of the chatbot's suggestions, a decision the court appeared to view with particular skepticism. It represents one of the first known cases in which a CEO's reliance on an AI chatbot to circumvent legal obligations became a central element of a court proceeding.

The case arrives at an awkward moment for AI in the enterprise. Companies are simultaneously urging employees to use AI tools to improve productivity while this ruling illustrates the risks of treating a language model as a strategic partner in high-stakes legal maneuvering. ChatGPT doesn't know your specific contractual obligations, doesn't have attorney-client privilege, and — as the $250 million outcome demonstrates — it can sound very confident while giving advice that a court will later describe as the foundation of a bad-faith scheme.

image.alt: A futuristic humanoid robot seated on a smooth floor, representing AI and automation technology
image.credit: Photo by Declan Sun on Unsplash

links.title: 404 Media: CEO ignores lawyers, asks ChatGPT how to void $250M contract, loses terribly in court
80.lv: Krafton CEO reportedly asked ChatGPT how to avoid paying Subnautica 2 developers
Hacker News discussion thread

stories.headline: Every Single Pair of Headphones Tested Contained Endocrine Disruptors
stories.hook: Researchers examined 81 headphone models and found toxic chemicals — including bisphenols, phthalates, and flame retardants — in every single one.
stories.body:
The ToxFree Life for All project, a collaboration of researchers from Central Europe, set out to understand what chemicals consumers are wearing against their heads for hours each day. They tested 81 headphone models — including budget products from online platforms like Temu and Shein as well as premium brands — analyzing 180 material samples across ear cushions, cables, headbands, and other components. The result was unambiguous and unsettling: every single product contained hazardous substances.

The three main classes of chemicals detected were bisphenols (including BPA and related compounds that can disrupt hormone signaling), phthalates (plasticizers linked to reproductive and developmental harm), and flame retardants (some of which are persistent in the environment and accumulate in body tissue). No one headphone model was clean. The researchers emphasize that the concentrations found in any individual product don't pose immediate acute danger, but that the cumulative and synergistic effects of chronic exposure — wearing headphones for extended periods, daily, over years — represent a longer-term concern that hasn't been adequately studied.

The report's most pointed conclusion is about consumer helplessness: there is currently no way for a buyer to select a safer headphone based on available information. Product labels don't disclose chemical composition, and the regulatory frameworks for consumer electronics haven't kept pace with growing evidence of harm from chronic low-level chemical exposure. The researchers are calling for mandatory material disclosure in consumer electronics and tighter EU and international standards. Until then, they suggest taking breaks from wearing headphones and avoiding direct skin contact with cable materials where possible.

image.alt: A large antenna or satellite dish structure rising above a dense green forest
image.credit: Photo by Shelymar Rosa Caraballo on Unsplash

links.title: Arnika: The Sound of Contamination — endocrine disruptors in headphones
Hacker News: Analysis of endocrine disruptors in headphones

sources: Arnika / ToxFree Life for All project

stories.headline: Meta's AI Just Mapped Every Tree Canopy on Earth at 1-Meter Resolution
stories.hook: A collaboration between Meta and the World Resources Institute produced canopy height maps so precise that individual trees are visible from space — and they could transform how we track forests.
stories.body:
Knowing how tall forest canopies are turns out to matter enormously for understanding carbon storage, biodiversity, water cycles, and the health of ecosystems. But measuring tree height at global scale has historically required expensive lidar surveys or coarse satellite estimates that miss crucial detail. A new collaboration between Meta AI and the World Resources Institute has changed that, producing Canopy Height Maps v2 — maps of tree height covering the entire planet at one-meter resolution.

The maps were built using DINOv3, Meta's self-supervised vision model, trained on a vast and geographically diverse dataset of satellite imagery combined with lidar reference data from across Europe, the United States, and beyond. The AI learned to infer tree height from visual cues in satellite images — shadow length, crown texture, spectral patterns — without needing a lidar instrument for every pixel. The improvement over the previous version is dramatic: the accuracy metric (R²) jumped from 0.53 to 0.86, and the maps now resolve individual trees that earlier versions blurred into indistinct patches of forest.

The applications are wide-ranging. National governments can use the maps to monitor deforestation at a scale and cost previously impossible. Urban planners can identify where tree canopies provide cooling and where they're absent. Conservation organizations can track restoration progress in real time. The EU's 3 Billion Tree Initiative, for instance, needs exactly this kind of monitoring tool to verify whether pledged plantings are actually growing. Meta has made the maps publicly available, a rare instance of large-scale AI infrastructure being deployed for open environmental use.

image.alt: Aerial view of lush green agricultural fields and cultivated crop plots seen from above
image.credit: Photo by Cosmin Andrei Buzamat on Unsplash

links.title: Meta AI: World Resources Institute and DINOv3 Canopy Height Maps v2
World Resources Institute: Forest monitoring tools

stories.headline: 8,000 People Helped Name a Deep-Sea Creature Found 3 Miles Down
stories.hook: A rare chiton discovered at the bottom of a Pacific trench sparked a global naming contest — and the result, 'Ferreiraella populi,' means 'of the people.'
stories.body:
Deep in the Izu-Ogasawara Trench, nearly 5,500 meters below the Pacific Ocean's surface, researchers recovered a remarkable specimen in 2024: a chiton — a segmented, armored mollusk — from a genus previously unknown at such depth. The creature had eight protective shell plates along its back, an iron-reinforced rasping tongue called a radula, and a small community of worms living near its tail, apparently feeding on its waste. Then the animal went viral.

Ze Frank, the creator of the popular YouTube series "True Facts" — whose narration of bizarre wildlife footage has attracted tens of millions of views — featured the chiton in one of his episodes. Researchers from the Senckenberg Research Institute in Frankfurt, recognizing an opportunity, opened a public naming contest. More than 8,000 suggestions flooded in from around the world, ranging from the creative to the deeply silly. Scientists evaluated the submissions using standard taxonomy rules — names must be in Latin or Greek, must not already be in use, and must ideally say something meaningful about the creature.

The chosen name, Ferreiraella populi, honors both a pioneering taxonomist (the genus name Ferreiraella was already established) and the public that brought it to widespread attention: populi, from the Latin for 'people.' It's believed to be one of the first formally described species whose scientific name explicitly commemorates the internet community that rallied to name it. The case has energized discussions within taxonomy about citizen science, the role of public engagement in scientific naming, and whether the tradition of obscure Latin names is actually helping or hindering public appreciation of Earth's extraordinary biodiversity.

image.alt: A colorful tropical fish swimming through clear dark blue ocean water among marine plants
image.credit: Photo by Oleksandr Sushko on Unsplash

links.title: Phys.org: The internet names a new deep-sea species of chiton
ScienceDaily: This weird deep-sea creature was named by thousands of people online
Discover Magazine: 8,000 suggestions later, a newly discovered deep-sea species gets its name

stories.headline: DiPT: The Obscure Psychedelic That Only Distorts Your Hearing
stories.hook: Most psychedelics produce vivid visual hallucinations — DiPT does something far stranger: it makes music sound a full octave lower than it actually is.
stories.body:
Among the hundreds of psychedelic compounds that researchers and chemists have explored over the past century, DiPT — N,N-diisopropyltryptamine — occupies a unique and peculiar niche. While most tryptamine psychedelics produce their effects primarily through visual hallucinations and altered perception of color, shape, and pattern, DiPT appears to act almost exclusively on auditory processing. Its primary effect is on pitch: sounds, voices, and music typically seem to shift approximately one octave downward, as though someone has slowed down reality's soundtrack without changing its tempo.

Users report that familiar pieces of music become deeply strange — the melody is recognizable, the rhythm unchanged, but everything sounds dragged into a lower register, with extra overtones and harmonic distortions that make instruments sound 'wrong' in a way that's difficult to describe. At higher doses, music can become so distorted that it loses any semblance of harmony. Paradoxically, many users also report an intensified emotional connection to music during the experience, sometimes describing it as profound or spiritually meaningful even as the sounds become objectively strange.

DiPT was first synthesized by pharmacologist Alexander Shulgin, who documented it in his 1997 book TiHKAL alongside hundreds of other psychedelics. Despite its fascinating specificity, it remains poorly understood scientifically — no human neuroimaging studies have yet examined how it alters auditory brain processing in real time. Its unusual profile makes it potentially valuable as a research tool: a compound that selectively disrupts one sensory modality could help neuroscientists map which circuits underlie normal pitch perception, though its legal status in most countries makes formal research difficult.

image.alt: A person silhouetted against the Milky Way galaxy, standing atop a dark hill beneath a stunning star-filled sky
image.credit: Photo by Rolv Skjærpe on Unsplash

links.title: Psychedelirium: The psychedelic that distorts hearing
Hacker News: DiPT discussion
Erowid: DiPT entry in TiHKAL

stories.headline: AI Chatbots Used as Therapists Violate Core Mental Health Ethics in 15 Distinct Ways
stories.hook: A Brown University study tested ChatGPT, Claude, and Llama as therapists against actual peer counselors — and found the AI models consistently failed at the things that make therapy safe.
stories.body:
As millions of people turn to AI chatbots for emotional support — sometimes explicitly for therapy-style conversations — Brown University researchers set out to rigorously measure how well these systems actually perform against the ethical standards of mental health care. The findings, published in early March 2026, identified 15 separate categories of ethical violations across the three major models tested: ChatGPT, Claude, and Meta's Llama.

The violations fell into five main areas. First, the chatbots showed poor contextual adaptation — offering generic advice without accounting for a person's specific situation, culture, or background. Second, they failed at therapeutic collaboration, sometimes steering conversations forcefully toward particular conclusions or reinforcing harmful beliefs the user expressed. Third, all models displayed what the researchers call 'deceptive empathy' — using phrases like 'I understand how you feel' in ways that mimic genuine care without any actual comprehension. Fourth, the models showed inconsistent responses across different demographic groups, suggesting embedded biases. Fifth, and most critically, they performed poorly in crisis situations — sometimes failing to recognize when a user's language indicated risk of self-harm, or responding to suicidal ideation with inappropriate responses.

The study's authors are careful to note that this doesn't mean AI has no role in mental health support. Access to professional therapy is severely limited by cost and availability, and AI tools might legitimately help fill some gaps. But the research argues that without regulatory frameworks — currently entirely absent — there is no accountability when an AI system's advice causes harm, unlike the professional licensing and oversight systems that govern human therapists. The researchers recommend that AI companies treat mental health use cases with the same regulatory seriousness as medical devices.

image.alt: A vintage laboratory setting with monitors and research equipment, representing medical and scientific research
image.credit: Photo by National Cancer Institute on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: ChatGPT as a therapist? New study reveals serious ethical risks
Brown University research on AI mental health ethics
APA Monitor: AI, neuroscience, and personalized mental health care (2026)

stories.headline: AI Businesses Are Faking It — and a Reckoning May Be Coming in Eight Months
stories.hook: Executives at software consulting firm Codestrap say companies adopting AI are measuring the wrong things, and code quality problems are silently accumulating that will surface later this year.
stories.body:
Dorian Smiley and Connor Deeks are founders of Codestrap, a software consultancy that has watched dozens of enterprises roll out AI coding tools over the past two years. In a frank assessment published this week, they argue that most of what's happening in enterprise AI isn't working as advertised — and that the problems have been hidden by metrics that look impressive but measure the wrong things.

The core issue is measurement. When companies track AI adoption, they typically count lines of code generated, pull requests merged, or the speed of initial development — all of which look great on a dashboard. But these metrics don't capture code quality, system reliability, or the crucial measure of how frequently deployments fail. Smiley points to a concrete example: an AI-generated SQLite implementation that passed all tests but ran 2,000 times slower than the original — a catastrophic performance failure that no superficial metric would catch. He predicts that organizations currently running heavy AI coding operations will start seeing these quality problems surface as production failures in about eight to nine months.

The financial pressures compounding this are significant. Customers are beginning to demand price discounts when they discover a project was built with AI. Insurance companies are pulling back from covering AI-generated code workflows, creating liability gaps. And at consulting firms where the profit motive runs directly counter to quality oversight, there's institutional pressure to replace senior engineers — the people who would catch these problems — with cheaper AI-assisted junior staff. The result, Smiley argues, is a system where the problems are real but all the incentives point away from fixing them before they become visible.

image.alt: A modern black robot with blue and orange accents, symbolizing advanced AI and automation technology
image.credit: Photo by Luan Fonseca on Unsplash

links.title: The Register: AI still doesn't work well, businesses are faking it, and a reckoning is coming
Hacker News discussion

stories.headline: Curiosity Found a Brand-New Mineral Hidden in Mars's Ancient Rocks
stories.hook: Buried in the sulfate-rich deposits of Gale Crater, NASA's Curiosity rover has detected a mineral that's never been documented on Mars before.
stories.body:
NASA's Curiosity rover has been picking its way across Gale Crater for over a decade, drilling into rock layers that record hundreds of millions of years of Martian geology. Its onboard chemistry instruments have catalogued dozens of minerals along the way, each one a clue to the planet's environmental history. But recent analysis of data from the rover's CheMin instrument — a miniaturized X-ray diffraction device — has turned up something unexpected: a mineral signature that doesn't match anything previously documented in the Martian rock record.

The mineral appears in the sulfate-bearing strata that Curiosity has been traversing in the upper part of Mount Sharp — rocks formed in conditions researchers believe were much drier and more acidic than earlier layers in the crater. Its precise identity and chemical formula are still being worked out, but its X-ray diffraction pattern is distinct from all known Martian minerals in the rover's reference library. Researchers are now working backward from its signature, comparing it to Earth minerals and laboratory-synthesized compounds, to determine what conditions would have produced it and what that implies about Mars's chemical history.

New minerals aren't just geochemical curiosities. Each unique mineral is a fingerprint of specific temperature, pressure, acidity, and water chemistry conditions at the time of its formation. If this new compound turns out to require acidic conditions with specific chemical concentrations to form, it constrains the range of environments that existed in Gale Crater during the period it crystallized — potentially adding a new chapter to the story of how Mars lost its habitability.

image.alt: A NASA Hubble Space Telescope projection map of Mars showing the planet's rusty orange and brown surface
image.credit: Photo by NASA Hubble Space Telescope on Unsplash

links.title: ScienceDaily: Space exploration latest news
NASA Mars exploration program — Curiosity rover
Phys.org: Mars mineral discoveries

stories.headline: The Free Software Foundation Is Threatening Anthropic: Open-Source Your AI or Share It Freely
stories.hook: The FSF found its own copyright was infringed in Anthropic's training data — and instead of taking money, it's demanding Anthropic release its model weights to the public.
stories.body:
The legal landscape around AI training data came into focus in late 2025 when Anthropic agreed to a $1.5 billion settlement in Bartz v. Anthropic — a class action lawsuit alleging the company had trained its Claude models on copyrighted books downloaded from Library Genesis and the Pirate Library Mirror without seeking permission. The settlement created a compensation fund for authors and publishers, and received preliminary court approval with a fairness hearing set for April 2026.

But the Free Software Foundation is taking a different approach. One of the works found in Anthropic's training dataset was "Free as in Freedom: Richard Stallman's Crusade for Free Software," written by Sam Williams and published under the GNU Free Documentation License — a license that requires derivatives or adaptations to also be freely shared. The FSF argues that using this work to train an LLM constitutes creating a derivative work, which means the entire model — weights, training configuration, and accompanying software — must be made freely available to users.

In a formal statement, the FSF rejected monetary compensation and instead issued a demand: Anthropic and other companies that trained on freely licensed material should make their complete models freely available, as the licenses on the training data require. The move is more philosophical than practically enforceable — the settlement process hasn't yet addressed the FSF's specific demands — but it crystallizes a debate that the entire AI industry will eventually have to face: if open-licensed material can be used to train commercially exploited AI models, what obligations does that create? The FSF's answer is unambiguous: the same obligations the license always imposed. Share it all, freely.

image.alt: A large satellite dish array against a clear sky, symbolizing communication and digital infrastructure
image.credit: Photo by Ambitious Studio* | Rick Barrett on Unsplash

links.title: FSF: The FSF doesn't usually sue for copyright — but when we do, we settle for freedom
The Register: FSF urges AI vendors to liberate LLMs
Authors Guild: What authors need to know about the Anthropic settlement

stories.headline: Archaeologists Found Ancient Shipwrecks Near a Forgotten Libyan Port — and They Hint at a Vast Lost Trade Network
stories.hook: Excavations near the ancient port city of Ptolemais in northeastern Libya have revealed multiple shipwrecks that shed new light on Mediterranean maritime trade thousands of years ago.
stories.body:
Ptolemais was one of the great cities of ancient Cyrenaica — a port on the northeastern Libyan coast that served as a hub of Mediterranean commerce during the Hellenistic and Roman periods. For centuries, scholars have known it primarily through literary references and architectural remains. Now, archaeologists from the University of Warsaw working near the historic port have uncovered the remains of several ancient shipwrecks lying in the shallow coastal waters, preserved by the dry conditions of the North African coastline.

The wrecks are still being assessed for dating and origin. Preliminary examination of their construction and cargo remains suggests they span multiple centuries, indicating that Ptolemais remained an active trading port across a broad stretch of the ancient Mediterranean world. Ceramics, metal fittings, and organic material recovered from the wreck sites point toward trade connections stretching from the Aegean to the Levant and potentially further east — consistent with the historical record of Cyrenaica as a junction between Greek, Egyptian, Roman, and African trade networks.

The find is particularly significant given how underexplored the Libyan coastline has been archaeologically. Decades of political instability have kept foreign excavation teams away from some of the most historically rich stretches of Mediterranean shoreline. The Warsaw team's work represents a careful resumption of fieldwork in a region that, according to archaeologists, contains some of the most intact ancient harbor infrastructure in the Mediterranean world — harbors whose cargo, if properly excavated, could significantly rewrite the economic history of the ancient world.

image.alt: Ancient stone ruins with tall palm trees rising behind them under a warm gradient sky
image.credit: Photo by Mr. Great Heart on Unsplash

links.title: Heritage Daily: Archaeology news
Archaeology Magazine: Latest discoveries 2026
Arkeonews: Recent archaeology

stories.headline: Scientists Found a Universal Temperature Law That Governs All Life on Earth
stories.hook: From microbes to lizards to trees, every organism on the planet follows the same fundamental mathematical relationship between temperature and performance — and now scientists have mapped it.
stories.body:
Temperature is the most universal constraint on life. Every organism on Earth — bacteria, fungi, insects, reptiles, plants, marine invertebrates — has a thermal performance curve: a range of temperatures over which it functions, with performance climbing from a cold baseline to an optimal peak and then declining sharply as heat stress sets in. What researchers didn't know was whether these curves, taken across the entire tree of life, follow any common mathematical pattern — or whether each species has its own unique thermal architecture.

A new analysis of thousands of species has found that they do share a common pattern. Across an enormous range of organisms, performance rises gradually with warming until an optimal temperature is reached, then falls off rapidly on the other side. The asymmetry — a gradual warm-up slope and a steep decline past the peak — is consistent across species that diverged billions of years ago and live in radically different environments. The exact numbers differ from species to species, but the shape of the curve follows a universal template.

The finding has immediate implications for climate modeling. Current predictions about how ecosystems will respond to warming have typically been built species-by-species, requiring enormous amounts of experimental data for each organism. A universal thermal performance template means researchers can now make more reliable extrapolations about how poorly-studied species will respond to temperature changes — filling in the blanks in ecosystem models for the thousands of species we've never been able to test in a lab. It also raises a profound evolutionary question: why has this particular curve shape been preserved across all of life? The answer likely lies in the deep biochemistry of enzymes, which lose function rapidly above their optimal temperatures — a constraint so fundamental that evolution has never found a way around it.

image.alt: A koala resting peacefully in the fork of a eucalyptus tree, representing Australia's wildlife

links.title: ScienceDaily: Top science news
Nature: Ecology and evolution
Phys.org: Biology and environment news

stories.headline: Scientists Discovered Bacteria That Move Without Their Propellers — and No One Knows How
stories.hook: Bacteria are supposed to swim using whip-like flagella, but researchers at Arizona State University found something that moves just fine without them.
stories.body:
Flagella are the bacterial world's version of propellers: hair-like appendages that rotate at high speed to push a bacterium through liquid. For decades, the flagellum has been considered the primary means of bacterial motility. Its elegant molecular motor is so well understood that it's become a standard example in biochemistry textbooks — and was famously cited in intelligent design arguments for its supposed irreducible complexity. Which makes it somewhat surprising that some bacteria, it turns out, don't need flagella to move at all.

Researchers at Arizona State University studying bacterial motility under high-powered microscopes found that certain bacterial cells move through liquid and across surfaces even when they lack functional flagella — either because the flagella were absent from birth or had been experimentally removed. The movement was slower and less directional than flagellar swimming, but unmistakably there. Analysis of the movement patterns ruled out passive drift from currents; the bacteria were actively propelling themselves.

The mechanism remains poorly understood. Current hypotheses include a kind of membrane-driven undulation, secretion of materials that create a chemical gradient the cell moves along, or a form of molecular crawling involving surface proteins not previously known to play a role in motility. The discovery matters because bacterial motility is closely tied to how bacteria spread, form biofilms, cause infections, and colonize new environments. If there's a secondary motility mechanism that antibiotics and other treatments don't target — because it was previously unknown — it could explain some cases of bacterial persistence that have puzzled microbiologists. It also means the flagellum, impressive as it is, may not be as essential to bacterial life as we thought.

image.alt: A USGS macro photograph of slime mold structures on water, representing microscopic biological life

links.title: ScienceDaily: Biology news — bacteria motility
Phys.org: Microbiology discoveries 2026
Arizona State University research news
